question: [File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: loadYaml()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a crafted YAML file via the CONFIG_PATH environment variable path traversal to override the db.host config key, redirecting all staking reward queries (including pending_reward lookups) to an attacker-controlled PostgreSQL instance, causing theft of unpaid staking reward data for all accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: loadYaml()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the yaml.load() call (which uses js-yaml's default schema) to inject a YAML document with special tags that override the db.password config key, gaining database access and directly reading the entity_stake.pending_reward column for all accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: loadYaml()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the extend(true, config, doc) deep merge in loadYaml() with a crafted YAML file containing prototype pollution payloads to override the response.limit config, removing pagination caps on the /api/v1/accounts/{id}/rewards endpoint and bulk-extracting all unpaid staking reward records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: loadYaml()] Can an unprivileged external user write a YAML config file to a world-writable CONFIG_PATH directory to set db.pool.maxConnections to an extreme value, exhausting the connection pool and causing a denial-of-service that prevents legitimate staking reward payouts from being processed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: loadYaml()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the silent error swallowing in the catch block of loadYaml() (logger.warn only, no rethrow) to supply a partially-valid YAML file that overrides only the db.host key, silently redirecting staking reward queries to an attacker-controlled host without alerting operators?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: setConfigValue()] Can an unprivileged external user inject an environment variable named HIERO_MIRROR_REST_DB_HOST pointing to an attacker-controlled database to redirect all staking_reward_transfer table queries, allowing the attacker to serve fabricated pending_reward values and steal reward claims?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: setConfigValue()] Can an unprivileged external user inject HEDERA_MIRROR_REST_DB_HOST (the deprecated prefix) via an environment variable, exploiting the automatic hedera-to-hiero migration in setConfigValue() to override db.host and redirect staking reward queries to a malicious database?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: setConfigValue()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the case-insensitive property matching in setConfigValue() (property === k.toLowerCase()) to inject an environment variable with mixed-case segments that matches a sensitive config key like db.password, gaining database credentials and directly querying pending_reward values?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: setConfigValue()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_REST_RESPONSE_LIMIT_DEFAULT to a very large integer via environment variable injection, bypassing the default pagination limit on /api/v1/accounts and bulk-enumerating all accounts with non-zero pending_reward values?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: setConfigValue()] Can an unprivileged external user inject HIERO_MIRROR_REST_QUERY_MAXTIMESTAMPRANGE to a very large duration string, bypassing the timestamp range restriction on /api/v1/accounts/{id}/rewards and extracting the full historical staking reward payout history for any account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: setConfigValue()] Can an unprivileged external user inject HIERO_MIRROR_REST_QUERY_MAXREPEATEDQUERYPARAMETERS to a very large value, enabling mass timestamp parameter injection on the /api/v1/accounts/{id}/rewards endpoint to extract arbitrarily large sets of staking reward transfer records in a single request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: setConfigValue()] Can an unprivileged external user inject HIERO_MIRROR_REST_DB_POOL_STATEMENTTIMEOUT to a very large value, disabling effective query timeouts on staking reward queries and enabling long-running enumeration attacks against the staking_reward_transfer table?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: setConfigValue()] Can an unprivileged external user inject HIERO_MIRROR_REST_DB_POOL_CONNECTIONTIMEOUT to zero or a negative value, causing parseDbPoolConfig() to throw an InvalidConfigError that crashes the REST service and prevents legitimate staking reward payouts from being queried?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: convertType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the isNaN() coercion in convertType() by injecting a numeric string for a boolean config key (e.g., HIERO_MIRROR_REST_RESPONSE_INCLUDEHOSTINLINK=1), causing unexpected truthy behavior that leaks internal host information in staking reward API responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: convertType()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a numeric string '0' for a boolean config key that controls staking reward access controls, exploiting convertType()'s numeric conversion path (+value returns 0, which is falsy) to disable a security check and gain unauthorized access to pending_reward data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: convertType()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a string like '0x10' for a numeric config key (e.g., response.limit.default), exploiting the isNaN() check which returns false for hex strings in some JS environments, causing convertType() to return an unexpected large integer that bypasses pagination limits on staking reward queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: convertType()] Can an unprivileged external user inject 'Infinity' as a value for a numeric config key like response.limit.max, exploiting convertType()'s !isNaN('Infinity') === true path to set the limit to Infinity, removing all caps on staking reward result set sizes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: convertType()] Can an unprivileged external user inject 'NaN' as a string value for a numeric config key, exploiting the fact that convertType() returns the string 'NaN' unchanged (isNaN('NaN') is true, so it falls through to the string return), causing downstream parseInt() or arithmetic to produce unexpected NaN behavior that bypasses staking reward query limits?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: loadEnvironment()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that loadEnvironment() iterates over ALL process.env entries without filtering, and if the attacker can inject environment variables into the process (e.g., via a shared hosting environment), override the db.host to redirect staking reward queries to a malicious server?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: loadEnvironment()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the order of operations in config.js (loadYaml runs before loadEnvironment) to inject an environment variable HIERO_MIRROR_REST_DB_PASSWORD that overrides the YAML-loaded database password, gaining credentials to directly query the staking_reward_transfer table?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: load()] Can an unprivileged external user control the CONFIG_PATH environment variable to point to an attacker-writable directory, causing load() to read a malicious application.yml that sets db.host to an attacker-controlled server and intercepts all pending_reward queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: load()] Can an unprivileged external user control the CONFIG_NAME environment variable to load an alternate config file (e.g., CONFIG_NAME=attacker) that overrides staking reward query limits, enabling bulk extraction of all accounts' pending_reward values?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: load()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that load() checks both .yml and .yaml extensions sequentially, and if both exist, the .yaml file's values overwrite the .yml file's values via extend(true, config, doc), to inject a second config file that overrides db credentials and steals staking reward data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: parseDbPoolConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user inject HIERO_MIRROR_REST_DB_POOL_MAXCONNECTIONS to a very large integer, causing parseDbPoolConfig() to accept it (parsed > 0), exhausting PostgreSQL connection limits and preventing the staking reward update process from writing new pending_reward values?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: parseDbPoolConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a floating-point string like '5.9' for db.pool.maxConnections, exploiting parseInt(value, 10) in parseDbPoolConfig() which truncates to 5 (valid), to set an unexpectedly low connection pool size that causes staking reward queries to queue and time out, denying service?
